title: practical english usage - Part 305
content:
In this job we're paid by the hour.

And one can pay by the hour, by the day etc.

The situation is getting more serious day by day. (= ... each day.) Note also day by day, hour by hour etc.

He worked by night and slept by day.

and by night (= during the day/night).

By can also be used to talk about time in the rather literary expressions by day 2 other     meanings For the difference between by and until, ►    613.6.

already inside the bank.

By the time that the guards realised what was happening the gang were I'll be in bed by the time you get home.

Before a verb, we  use by the time (that).

By the end of the meal, everybody was drunk.

By can also suggest the idea of 'progress up to a particular time’ .

(= tonight or before) 'Can I borrow your car? 'Yes, but I must have it back by tonight.' I'll be home by five o'clock. (= at or before five) By can mean ‘ not later than’ .

1  not   later    than 414 by:  time For except, ►    456.   For but as a conjunction and ellipsis after but, ►    276.

Note: but is usually pronounced /bət/, not /bʌt/  (►  315).

She is but a child.

In older English, but was used to mean ‘ only’ , but this is now very unusual.

4 but    meaning       'only' The train was cancelled, so I had no alternative but to take a taxi.

Infinitives with to are used after no alternative/choice!option but...

I can't help but wonder what's going to happen to us all.

One cannot (help) but admire his courage. (= One has to admire ...) especially common in American English.

‘ can’ t help .. .ing’  (►  418). Cannot but... is very formal; can't help but... is + infinitive               without Can't (help) but          to is sometimes used with the meaning of That child does nothing but watch TV. (does... watch) She's not interested in anything but skiing, (interested in .. . skiing) normally without to.

The  verb form after but usually depends on what came before. Infinitives are 3 verbs     after    but Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary •  415  by and near ‘ Alice called this morning.' ‘ You mean she came here or she phoned?’ causes confusion.

Call (with no object) can mean both 'telephone’  and 'visit’ . This sometimes 417 call to kill him’ .) He was killed with a heavy stone. (This means 'Somebody used a stone and killed him’ .) He was killed by a heavy stone. (This could mean 'A stone fell Compare: We   generally prefer with to refer to a tool or instrument used by somebody.

My car was damaged by a falling branch.

I was interviewed by three directors.

responsible for the action (►  58).

In passive clauses, by introduces the agent - the person or thing that is 2  passive      clauses By is also used to refer to means of transport (by bus, by train, etc), ►     142.1.

We can't get it out without a screwdriver.

- We'll have to get it out with a screwdriver.

It's difficult to get her to listen without shouting.

- I got her to listen by shouting.

Without is the opposite of both by and with in these cases. Compare: ‘ No you didn't. You got there with your wife's money.' -   7 got where I am by hard work.’ not I killed the spider with a shoe. (     .. .by a shoe.) - I killed the spider by hitting it. (Note the -ing form after by.) talk about a tool or other object - what we  use to get a result. Compare: We   use by to talk about an action - what we  do to get a result. We   use with to is an important difference.

By and with can both be used to say how  somebody does something, but there 1  the   difference 416 by   (method,     agent)    and   with     (tools,   etc) We live by the sea. (We can see it.) We live near the sea. (perhaps five kilometres away) something that is near you. Compare: By means 'just at the side of'; something that is by you may be closer than 415  by   and   near vocabulary •  419   care: take care (of), care (about) and care for I don't much  care for strawberries.

in modern English.

Another meaning is 'like’  or 'be fond of, but this is not very common He spent years caring for his sick mother.

Care for can be used to mean 'look after’ .
